arbitrated.notes:
Prior meeting's carry was a modest bearish-duration (higher yields) tilt built mainly on inflation and a firming term premium. This meeting largely confirms that lean but with some softening at the margins. Inflation itself is stale (16 days old) but was a strong "up" call (0.62) at formation and nothing here contradicts it directly — no fresher inflation or inflation-expectations read undercuts it (inflation_expectations is flat but low conviction and a different, softer measure). Term premium is fresh and up (0.40), consistent with and reinforced by balance_sheet also fresh and up (0.35, i.e., runoff continuing/less accommodative), which closes the gap flagged between term_premium and balance_sheet — both point the same direction, supply-driven upward pressure on term premium is corroborated rather than undercut. Financial conditions are flat/low-conviction, so the flight-to-quality offset flagged as a term-premium gap does not materialize this month — no evidence of risk-off flows compressing premia. Labor tightness is stale and flat, a genuine non-signal, correctly discounted. Curve_slope is out of scope for this seat (curve shape, not level) and I exclude it entirely.

Net: the level case for higher yields is intact and modestly reinforced — inflation pressure (stale but unrebutted), term premium rising on real supply/composition grounds now corroborated by balance-sheet runoff continuing, with financial conditions neutral rather than offsetting. This is not a stronger call than before, but it's not weaker either; the gap that could have flipped term_premium (financial-conditions-driven flight to quality) didn't show up. I hold the existing modest up-yield duration position rather than resize meaningfully, given panel is directionally consistent but no driver strengthened enough to add conviction, and inflation's staleness caps how hard I lean.

discounted.why: Stale (23 days) and flat conviction; contributes no directional signal.
Out of scope for level view — curve shape belongs to another pod's seat.

arbitrated.falsifier: A fresh inflation or inflation-expectations print surprising meaningfully lower, or financial conditions tightening sharply into flight-to-quality that compresses term premium.

risks.text: Stale inflation call could reverse on next CPI print, removing the main upward driver.
A dovish shift in balance-sheet runoff pace would remove the term-premium supply support.
Sudden risk-off could still trigger flight-to-quality that financial_conditions hasn't yet flagged.
Panel is mostly moderate-conviction, single-meeting-fresh calls; level signal is not decisively strong.

trade.rationale: Hold modest bearish-duration tilt (higher yields), long-end weighted given term-premium/supply story; unchanged from prior meeting as panel confirms but doesn't strengthen the call.

arbitrated.notes: The panel is coherent and, if anything, has strengthened versus last meeting. Inflation itself is 13 days stale but corroborated by a fresh, high-conviction inflation-expectations read (up, 0.65) — that's a gap-closer, not a contradiction, so I keep a firm upward inflation view. Financial conditions flipped from a near-flat +0.05 last time to a clearly loosening (risk-on) +0.65 this meeting — that's a real move and pushes against duration richening, i.e., consistent with higher yields as risk appetite firms rather than a flight-to-quality bid. Balance sheet continues to run off (+0.45, up = shrinking), which mechanically adds duration supply and pushes term premium and yields higher. Term premium's own call is a more modest +0.30 up, and it's explicitly missing both the balance-sheet supply read and the financial-conditions read that would have corroborated it — but since both of those inputs came in supportive (shrinking balance sheet, easier financial conditions), I read term premium as understated and lean on the better-informed cross-read to nudge it up in my synthesis. Labor tightness is stale (20 days) and flat/low-conviction — I abstain rather than let it anchor the level. Curve slope is out of scope for this seat (curve shape belongs elsewhere) and I set it aside. Net: inflation, financial conditions, and balance-sheet runoff are all pushing nominal yields higher, term premium is a supportive tailwind, and nothing in this panel argues for a reversal. This confirms and modestly reinforces the standing long-yield (short-duration) position rather than flipping it.

arbitrated.notes: Panel broadly reaffirms last meeting's tilt but with some softening. Inflation itself is stale (16 days) yet still shows a strong "up" conviction (0.75) — I keep it as a background support for higher yields but discount its freshness. Financial conditions (fresh, 0.60 up) and term premium (fresh, 0.40 up) both point to tighter conditions and higher term premium, consistent with upward yield pressure, and term premium's own gap note (missing balance_sheet input) is not fatal since balance_sheet itself is now flat/neutral (0.40 conviction, no directional lean) — so the runoff-driven supply story that previously supported higher term premium is weaker than before. Balance_sheet flipping from a supportive +0.45 lean to a flat call is the one real change from last meeting: it removes part of the structural case for higher long-end yields via reduced supply pressure. Labor_tightness (down, i.e., loosening) is stale-ish (23 days) but directionally consistent with softer labor market, which is modestly disinflationary and yield-negative — a small offset. Inflation_expectations (new driver, not previously tracked) shows a "down" call (0.45) which cuts against the inflation driver's "up" call — this is a genuine split on the inflation axis, weakening the inflation-side case for higher yields. Curve_slope is out of scope for this seat. Net: financial conditions and term premium still lean toward modestly higher yields, but the flip in balance_sheet to flat and the fresh inflation-expectations pushback meaningfully soften the conviction from last meeting. I resize the position down rather than fully reverse, reflecting a still-net-hawkish but less confident panel than before.

arbitrated.notes:
Since last meeting the picture has softened toward a more balanced level view, though it hasn't reversed. Term premium is now the strongest, freshest signal (formed this meeting, conviction 0.55, up), reinforced by financial_conditions (fresh, up 0.35) and inflation_expectations (fresh, up 0.35) — three live reads all pointing to higher yields via risk premium and expectations channels. Against that, inflation itself is stale (15 days) and now points down (-0.35), a flip from my prior +0.50 call, but the panel flags a real gap: inflation lacks the inflation_expectations input, and inflation_expectations (fresh) says up — so the stale inflation-down read is likely missing the expectations context and I discount it rather than let it drive the level. Labor_tightness is stale (22 days) and flat, so I treat it as a non-signal, consistent with last time. Balance_sheet is fresh but flat (conviction 0.45 on "flat" itself, i.e., no change in stance) — no supply-pressure revival, so no incremental upward push from that channel; I hold it near zero. Curve_slope is out of my mandate (shape, not level) and I set it aside entirely.

Net: the fresher, better-informed drivers (term premium, financial conditions, inflation expectations) lean yields higher, while the countervailing inflation signal is stale and likely understated given the missing expectations context. This isn't a stronger case than last meeting, but it hasn't reversed either — if anything the fresh evidence marginally reinforces the modest upward tilt I already held. I keep the position essentially where it was, without adding conviction, since the incremental evidence is confirmatory rather than a genuine new catalyst.

arbitrated.notes: Since last meeting the picture has shifted meaningfully toward lower yields, and I need to respect that. Balance_sheet's own measurement (its "down" call, conviction 0.75) means the stock of holdings/pace is reported as falling — but note the inversion: a shrinking balance sheet is a QT-like force that has historically pushed term premium and yields UP, which is what drove my prior long-duration lean. This meeting's balance_sheet analyst is more convicted on that same direction than last time, so the QT-supply-pressure story is intact, not reversed. However, financial_conditions has now flipped/strengthened to "down" (i.e., financial conditions loosening/easing, conviction 0.55) versus my prior weak +0.05 read — that's a real move and it argues for tighter risk premia and typically lower yields via reduced risk-off pressure, but "financial conditions down" is ambiguous language here; given the panel's own framing (loosening conditions can also signal easier credit/equity tailwinds sometimes correlating with a bit less duration demand), I read this as modestly yield-lowering given curve_slope and financial_conditions both call "down" with meaningful conviction. Term_premium itself is now flat (down from +0.35 conviction previously), and importantly flags that it was never given the balance_sheet or financial_conditions inputs — a real gap. Given balance_sheet's strong reading, I lean on it over term_premium's stale-feeling flat call, since term_premium admits missing the very input that should move it. Inflation reads down (mild), labor_tightness flat, both consistent with previous, unremarkable. Net: the QT/balance-sheet force still argues for higher term premium and higher yields, but financial_conditions and curve_slope softening this month, plus term_premium's own flat print, tempers my prior long-duration conviction. I keep the position but size it down given the increased disagreement and the term_premium gap.

Balance sheet conviction firmed to -0.75 (continued/accelerating runoff — bearish for yields via reduced Fed demand). Financial conditions is now -0.65, i.e., conditions are loosening (easier financial conditions), which is a fresh, live read and it points toward lower yields, not higher — this is the key change from last time when I had financial_conditions at -0.60 but was reading it as tightening pressure. Inflation is down (-0.45, though 16 days stale) and inflation_expectations also down (-0.45, fresh) — corroborating disinflationary signal, pulling yields down. Term premium call is now flat/weak (0.25 conviction) versus my prior +0.45 — the term premium story has cooled, undercutting my prior upside case. Labor tightness is flat and stale (23 days), so I give it no directional weight. Curve slope is out of my seat.

Netting these: balance sheet still argues for higher yields (supply/QT pressure), but that is now substantially offset by softer term premium, disinflationary CPI/breakevens, and looser financial conditions all pointing lower. The previous meeting's mild long-yield/curve-up position was built on a firmer term premium and tighter financial conditions read that has now weakened. This is a genuine gap-closing shift, not noise — I should scale down my long-yield tilt toward closer to flat, keeping only a small residual reflecting balance-sheet/supply persistence.

Balance sheet runoff continues to shrink (conviction 0.78, fresh) — this is a yield-UP force (less duration absorption, more supply pressure on term premium) even though its own headline measurement is "down." Term premium itself is now called UP (0.35, fresh) for the first time after two meetings of no-view, though it's built without balance_sheet or financial_conditions inputs — both of which are relevant and one (balance sheet) is falling sharply, which should reinforce the term-premium-up case via added supply pressure. That's a gap I can close in the term-premium analyst's favor: I lean into the up-call being directionally right, perhaps understated. Financial conditions have cooled from tightening-down to flat (0.35, weak conviction) — no longer a strong yields-down force, effectively neutral now versus last meeting's -0.65 easing call. Inflation is stale (13 days old) and its own call is down; I discount it but don't let it anchor the read. Labor tightness stale (20 days), flat, no view net. Inflation_expectations (not my labeled driver but informative) up somewhat, consistent with term premium up. Net: balance sheet outflow (up-yield pressure) + term premium turning up + financial conditions no longer easing = a mild firming in the case for higher yields versus last month, where I was moderately bearish on yields. This is enough to nudge my long-yield tilt slightly higher, not reverse the position wholesale — the panel isn't unanimous (curve_slope down, inflation down are yield-down leaning) but the fresh, more-informed drivers (balance sheet, term premium) point up. I increase conviction modestly and size up slightly the existing modest long-duration-up tilt.

arbitrated.notes: Last meeting I ran a modest long-yield tilt built on a shrinking balance sheet (term-premium up) and softening inflation, with financial conditions neutral. This meeting flips two of those three pillars. Balance sheet reading is now "up" (assets growing, +0.45) — the opposite sign from last time's -0.78 call — which is disinflationary for the term premium/supply channel and argues for lower, not higher, yields from that channel. Term premium itself is now called down (-0.35) rather than up, directly reversing the prior driver that anchored my long-yield trade. Financial conditions, previously a non-view, now reports a clear tightening call (up, 0.65) — that's the one piece that argues for higher yields (tighter conditions typically coincide with higher policy-sensitive rates or reflect a hawkish repricing), but it's in tension with the term-premium analyst's own gap-flag that a flight-to-quality could be depressing yields, which would actually go with tightening financial conditions in a risk-off sense — muddying the read further. Inflation stays down (-0.55, though 16 days stale) and labor stays flat/stale. Given balance-sheet and term-premium have both reversed against my carried position, and financial conditions is the only fresh driver still pointing up, I no longer have three aligned pillars supporting a long-yield tilt. The disagreement across fresh drivers (balance_sheet down-yield, term_premium down-yield, financial_conditions up-yield) is now a genuine split rather than confirmation. This is exactly the kind of gap-driven reversal the panel flagged (term_premium lacking balance_sheet/financial_conditions context) — I should not hold the same long-yield trade on materially weaker, now-conflicting fresh evidence. I am cutting size sharply toward flat, keeping only a token long-yield lean given financial conditions' strength, rather than fully reversing to short on a single fresh split.

Balance sheet: runoff continuing, conviction on shrinkage actually strengthened (0.78 vs 0.72 last time) — this is a persistent upward pressure on yields via reduced Fed demand/duration absorption, but it was already in my prior. Since it's still ungrounded in those two drivers rather than freshly informed by them, I don't treat this as the flip I specified; the term_premium call remains a live but under-informed read, not a fresh confirmation. Inflation is stale (16 days) and flat/up at modest conviction — not fresh enough to lean on for a level shift; labor_tightness is stale (23 days) and flat — no information content, effectively a null. Curve_slope and inflation_expectations are outside my mandate (curve, real/nominal) but inflation_expectations flat with decent conviction weakly corroborates that inflation isn't running away, tempering the stale inflation-up read. Net: balance-sheet-driven upward pressure is intact and slightly firmer, partially offset by financial-conditions easing; term premium's upward call is real but methodologically unanchored to the two drivers that matter most for it. This nets to a mild-to-moderate net upward yield pressure, but not decisively stronger than before — I'll size up my duration-short bias slightly rather than reverse it, since nothing here contradicts my prior thesis, it just firms one leg (balance sheet) while softening another (financial conditions).

arbitrated.notes: Since last meeting the panel has shifted materially on the one driver I was leaning on most: term_premium has gone from +0.40 (rising, my main upward-yield force) to flat (0.30 conviction, formed fresh this meeting). That was explicitly my stated flip trigger — I said if the combined term-premium read (now formed alongside financial_conditions/balance_sheet context, though the gaps note it still lacks those inputs directly) flips to flat/down, I'd cut the position. It has flipped to flat. Meanwhile balance_sheet stays firmly negative (runoff continuing, -0.78, fresh) and financial_conditions has gotten more negative and fresher (-0.60 vs -0.45, formed this meeting) — both are disinflationary/yield-suppressing via the liquidity and duration-supply channel in the intuitive sense: shrinking balance sheet is measured negative here but pushes yields up; tightening financial conditions (measured down, i.e. tightening) also net pushes yields down via demand destruction — these two continue to point in a consistent low-yield direction on net (balance sheet mechanically supportive of higher term premium/yields, financial conditions tightening pushing yields down, roughly offsetting me toward neutral on that axis, as before). Inflation (stale, 16 days, +0.55 up) and labor_tightness (stale, 23 days, +0.45 up) both point toward higher yields but are aging and not refreshed — I discount them per staleness rules. Inflation_expectations (fresh, down 0.55) actually undercuts the inflation upside call, adding a fresh disinflationary cross-read that the stale inflation read didn't have. Net: my term-premium anchor for the short position has weakened notably, the panel is more mixed than last time, and disinflationary cross-currents (financial conditions, inflation expectations) are fresher than the inflationary ones. I am cutting size on the short-yields (long duration price / short yield) position rather than reversing, since the panel didn't flip decisively — it flattened, which argues for reduced conviction, not a reversal.

arbitrated.notes:
Since last meeting the picture has shifted modestly toward higher yields, but not dramatically. Balance sheet runoff continues (down, conviction 0.80, fresh) — this is a persistent upward force on yields via reduced Fed demand/duration absorption, consistent with last meeting. Term premium has flipped from a no-view to a fresh "up" call (0.55) — this is the clearest change in the panel and pushes toward higher long yields, particularly given curve_slope also reports "up" (steepening bias, though that's not my seat). Financial conditions has cooled from my prior -0.60 (tightening) read to a fresh "flat" (0.35 conviction) — this removes one of the two legs that justified my prior duration-long lean, since financial conditions easing/staying flat doesn't argue for lower yields anymore. Inflation is stale (15 days) at a mild "up" 0.30, unchanged in direction from before, and lacks inflation_expectations input — flagged gap, but inflation_expectations itself reports flat, tempering conviction. Labor tightness is stale (22 days), flat, weaker signal than before.

Net: the previously-carried modest short-duration (long-yield... wait, my position was negative-weighted, meaning I was positioned for yields to fall) case rested on financial conditions tightening and balance sheet shrinking pushing yields up, offset by inflation/labor calls. Now financial conditions has gone flat, and term premium has turned clearly higher — both of these argue AGAINST my prior "yields falling" tilt. The named gaps (term_premium lacking balance_sheet and financial_conditions context) don't overturn the term_premium call since balance_sheet is itself falling (consistent, supply-side reinforcing) and financial_conditions is flat (neutral, not contradicting). This is enough of a turn — a driver that reversed (financial_conditions) plus a new fresh driver (term_premium) — to flip my position from modestly short-yield to modestly long-yield (higher rates), sized small given persistent disagreement (curve/inflation stale/flat).

arbitrated.notes: Net read is still modestly higher yields, but conviction should come down slightly versus last meeting because the panel has softened/reversed on a couple of the drivers that were doing the work before. Balance sheet: fresh, clear call that runoff continues (down, conviction 0.80) — this still pushes yields up via reduced Fed demand for duration, consistent with my last call. Term premium: fresh read now shows up but only 0.40, versus my prior 0.55 down-weighted view of the same direction; the panel flags it's missing the balance-sheet input, and since balance-sheet is now clearly negative (runoff continuing), that should if anything reinforce term premium rising on supply grounds — so I keep it rising, but note the gap makes it a shakier, dependent read. Financial conditions flipped from my prior no-view to a fresh "up" tightening call at 0.55 — this is a genuine change from last meeting and cuts against duration-short thinking somewhat (tightening financial conditions argue for lower yields prospectively, or at least caps the upside). Inflation is stale (16 days) and modestly down; labor_tightness is stale (23 days) and up (tighter labor, hawkish for yields) — these roughly offset and neither is fresh enough to lean on hard. Curve_slope and inflation_expectations are out of my mandate (curve, real/nominal split) so I set them aside. On net: balance-sheet runoff and rising term premium still argue for higher yields, but freshly-tightening financial conditions is a genuine new headwind that wasn't there last meeting, disagreement across the panel is real, and the term-premium read has an acknowledged gap. This argues for trimming size on the long-yields position rather than reversing or adding to it.

arbitrated.notes:
Since last meeting the picture has softened materially on the drivers I leaned on for a higher-yield call. Balance sheet: the analyst now reports runoff itself is DOWN (conviction 0.80) — i.e., the balance-sheet is shrinking more slowly / stabilizing than before, which is a reversal from my prior -0.80 read and removes the biggest source of upward term-premium/supply pressure I was pricing. Financial conditions: now DOWN (easier) at 0.45, a clear flip from my prior +0.55 (tightening) call — easier conditions argue for lower, not higher, yields. Term premium: flat at 0.35, down from my prior +0.40 view, and it explicitly lacks the financial_conditions and balance_sheet inputs — but since both of those inputs have now moved toward "easier/slower runoff," the gap, if closed, would likely push term_premium's own read toward flat-to-down rather than up. Inflation: down at 0.35 (fresh CPI-cycle read, though formed 15 days ago) and inflation_expectations flat — both consistent, no upside inflation pressure. arbitrated.notes:
The panel has softened relative to last meeting. Balance sheet remains the loudest and freshest voice: runoff continues (conviction 0.72, "down" in balance-sheet size), which is a genuine upward pressure on yields via reduced Fed absorption of duration, consistent with my prior read. However, term_premium — which last meeting I read as rising (+0.45) partly on the expectation that supply pressure would show through — has now gone flat at only 0.30 conviction, and it explicitly flags that it lacks the balance_sheet input needed to translate runoff into a supply-premium call. That gap, plus term_premium's own flat call, undercuts the strength of my prior up-yield thesis: the transmission from balance-sheet shrinkage to higher term premium is not confirmed by the analyst who should own that link. Financial conditions are flat (0.40 conviction) — no tightening impulse to add duration-up pressure. Inflation is now leaning down (-0.35, though 16 days stale) and labor tightness is flat (also 23 days stale). Neither offers a strong pull in either direction, and both are aging. Curve_slope and inflation_expectations are outside my remit but corroborate a "flat" macro backdrop.

Net: the single strongest, freshest driver (balance sheet runoff) still points to modestly higher yields, but its main transmission channel (term premium) has weakened and now disclaims the very link I needed. Inflation's downward lean and flat labor market temper any independent upward inflation pressure. This is not a reversal-worthy shift — no driver has flipped sign, just term_premium's conviction fading and a gap surfacing. I keep a small, slightly reduced long-yield tilt rather than abandon the position.

Financial conditions flipped: I had +0.35 (tightening) last time, but this meeting the FC analyst calls "down" at 0.55 conviction — meaning financial conditions are loosening/easing, which is a yield-down force (less restrictive, but also can mean less flight-to-quality tightening). Term premium, which I had at +0.45 conviction last time (rising, pushing yields up), is now flat at only 0.25 conviction, formed fresh this meeting — that's a real reversal of the term premium impulse and directly triggers part of my stated falsifier. Inflation is stale (15 days) and only mild +0.40 lean up — I discount it somewhat given staleness but it's not contradicted. Labor tightness is stale (22 days) and flat/weak — no new information, discount toward abstention. Curve_slope and inflation_expectations are outside my mandate (curve/real splits) so I ignore them for the level call.

Net: the two strongest fresh reads — term premium cooling and financial conditions loosening — both pull yields down, partially offset by continued balance-sheet drawdown (up pressure) and lukewarm-but-stale inflation. This is enough of a shift from last meeting's up-tilt setup to trim my short-duration lean, not reverse it. My conviction in a rising-yield world has weakened noticeably; I move toward a smaller, lower-conviction level position rather than holding the previous size.

Financial conditions: fresh read at -0.60, i.e. financial conditions loosening/easing (down move), consistent with last meeting's -0.55. This is disinflationary/duration-supportive — pushes yields down as looser conditions reduce term compensation needed. No reversal here, so my prior falsifier condition (FC reverting to upward push) has NOT been triggered.

Term premium: flat, weak conviction (0.30), fresh. No push either way — genuinely undercuts any big directional call from this driver, consistent with my prior "no view."

Inflation: "up" 0.45 but stale (16 days) — some tension against inflation_expectations (flat, fresh, 0.30) which is better-informed and contemporaneous. I discount the stale inflation print in favor of the fresher flat expectations read.

Labor tightness: flat, stale (23 days), low conviction — no basis for a view.

Net: financial conditions easing and term premium flat outweigh balance-sheet-driven supply pressure and stale inflation upside. This keeps my modest yields-down lean intact, not reversed but not strengthened — the panel didn't hand me a clean trigger to flip or size up meaningfully.

arbitrated.notes: Since last meeting the picture has shifted meaningfully toward higher yields, and I need to respect that. Term premium has flipped from my prior easing read (-0.40) to a fresh "up, 0.55" call — this is the single biggest change and it's formed at this meeting, not stale. Balance sheet continues to shrink (down 0.65, even more conviction than before), which mechanically adds duration supply and pushes yields up via term premium/supply channel — consistent with, and reinforcing, the term premium analyst's own call. Financial conditions is still easing/loosening (down 0.45) but conviction is now more moderate than my prior -0.60, and this driver would ordinarily lean yields down, opposing the balance-sheet/term-premium supply story — a genuine tension, but I weight it less because term premium's fresh call plus the balance sheet gap flag (term premium wasn't shown balance sheet data) suggests the analyst may be underweighting the very supply pressure that's building. Inflation is stale (16 days) but points up at 0.60, and is now corroborated by a fresh inflation_expectations read (up, 0.35) — that convergence across a stale and a fresh source raises confidence the inflation impulse is genuine, adding further upward pressure on yields. Labor tightness is stale and flat/low-conviction — sidelined. Curve_slope isn't my seat. Netting this out: the two fresh, high-conviction drivers (term premium up, balance sheet down/shrinking) both point to higher yields, corroborated by inflation and inflation expectations; only financial conditions pulls the other way and its conviction eased since last time. This is enough of a shift from my prior easing call to reverse toward a modest yields-up view, but disagreement (FC vs TP/BS) argues for modest sizing, not a big reversal.

Net: balance-sheet runoff (up) and term premium (up) still outweigh a newly-loosening financial-conditions signal (down) and stale inflation (up but discounted). The level case for higher yields is intact but not strengthened — if anything slightly softened by the financial-conditions reversal. I hold my long-duration-yields (short bonds) position but trim conviction slightly given the offset and staleness discounts rather than resize aggressively.

arbitrated.notes:
The panel has moved meaningfully against my prior read. Last meeting I had balance_sheet strongly falling (-0.75, i.e., QT continuing to drain, pushing yields up) but weighed against a firming financial-conditions call (+0.40, loosening, yields down) and falling inflation/term-premium (both yields-down). Net was a small yields-up tilt.

This meeting: balance_sheet call is "down" at 0.55 — consistent in direction with before but reduced conviction (own-measurement falling, i.e., continued balance-sheet reduction — this still pushes yields UP on net, since the seat's convention is own-measurement direction, not rate effect). Financial_conditions flipped to "up" at 0.55 — this is a fresh, live read replacing my previous +0.40 view, and now conviction is stronger. Since FC-up (loosening/risk-on conditions typically read as financial conditions index rising = tighter, but per prior convention I treated +0.40 as loosening/yields-down; here I'll keep consistency: FC "up" call at higher conviction reinforces the same directional lean as before, now stronger). Term_premium flipped from my prior -0.45 (falling) to "down" at 0.35 — actually consistent (falling), though gaps noted: term_premium lacks balance_sheet and financial_conditions inputs, so this read is somewhat under-informed and should be given less standalone weight. Inflation flipped from my prior -0.45 (falling) to "up" at 0.35, but this call is 16 days stale — I discount it moderately, though it's directionally opposite my prior and worth flagging. Labor_tightness stayed flat but is 23 days stale — abstain-leaning, no strong lever either way. Curve_slope isn't my seat (curve, not level) but its "down" call plus gaps versus term_premium/financial_conditions suggests the long end is not being pushed by supply/QT alone.

Net: balance_sheet continues to argue for higher yields (QT still draining), while term_premium and financial_conditions (now firmer/live) both lean yields-down, and inflation (stale) ticks toward up. The offsetting forces roughly cancel, slightly tilted toward the yields-up side given balance_sheet's persistence and inflation's fresh (if stale) upward tick, but conviction is lower given genuine three-way disagreement and unresolved gaps. I trim my prior small long-yield tilt down toward roughly flat/slightly cautious, keeping the same sign but at reduced size given weaker net directional edge.

Last meeting I ran a modest bull-flattener-ish long-duration tilt (small negative weight on both legs) driven by a shrinking balance sheet, easing term premium, and no financial-conditions tightening, with inflation/labor as partial offsets. This meeting largely confirms that structure but softens the case for further duration-buying. Term premium also flattened to near-zero conviction (0.20, flat) from -0.35 — the compression thesis has stalled. Financial conditions stay flat/neutral but with firmer conviction (0.55), reinforcing no easing or tightening impulse from that channel. Inflation and labor_tightness are stale (15 and 22 days old) but both still point up (0.55 and 0.45), which is unchanged in direction from last time and was already my basis for a positive inflation/labor conviction — I keep those calls but weight them cautiously given staleness. Inflation_expectations (a curve/breakeven read, not my seat) shows down, but it explicitly lacks the inflation and financial_conditions inputs I do have, so I discount it as under-informed relative to my read.

Net: the two supports for my prior modest long-duration lean (shrinking balance sheet, falling term premium) have weakened to near-neutral, while the offsetting inflationary/labor pressures persist unchanged. This tips the net level read from mildly lower-yields to roughly neutral-to-slightly-higher. I trim my long-duration position toward flat rather than fully reversing, since no single driver flipped hard against me — this is a fade of conviction, not a reversal signal.

Financial_conditions and term_premium both come in flat with modest conviction (0.55 and 0.25) — genuinely fresh, but they carry no directional push either way, so they neither reinforce nor reverse the tilt. Inflation and labor_tightness remain the same as last meeting but are now flagged stale (46 and 53 days) — I discount their weight but don't discard them entirely since no fresher inflation/labor print has arrived to contradict them; the direction they implied (higher price pressure, tighter labor) is unchanged, just not fresh. arbitrated.notes: Since last meeting the mix has shifted meaningfully softer on the rate axis. Balance sheet now reads as a pivot toward slower runoff/less contraction (down, 0.45) rather than the steady drain I had last time — this is disinflationary for term premium/supply pressure and pulls against my prior +0.55 balance-sheet-driven bid for higher yields. Term premium itself is now called down (0.35) directly by its analyst, even though that analyst lacks balance_sheet and financial_conditions inputs — but its direction agrees with where those two drivers are now pointing anyway, so the gap doesn't undercut the read, it reinforces it. Financial conditions are flat (0.45), a live read that argues for no independent tightening/loosening impulse. Inflation is flat but stale (15 days) — I downgrade its weight; inflation_expectations (fresh, down 0.40) is the better-informed, live read here and should dominate over the stale inflation call, consistent with the panel's own gap logic. Labor tightness is up (0.55) but stale at 22 days — a full jobs-cycle old — so I discount it toward abstention; it's the one holdout for higher yields and it's the least fresh voice on the panel. Net: three fresh, converging signals (balance sheet pivot, term premium down, inflation expectations down, financial conditions flat) outweigh one stale hawkish labor read and one stale flat inflation read. This flips my level view from the prior mild higher-yield lean to a mild lower-yield lean. I'm resizing modestly rather than aggressively given the panel still shows real disagreement (curve_slope flat, financial_conditions flat) and only moderate individual convictions.

arbitrated.notes: The panel has shifted meaningfully against my prior easing-yields read. Balance_sheet, which I previously called falling (runoff continuing, pushing yields down via less supply pressure... actually I had it at -0.45 meaning balance sheet shrinking, which is yield-positive via reduced Fed absorption... wait, I need to be consistent: I read balance_sheet own-measurement falling last time; now it's called UP with conviction 0.60 — a clear reversal in the driver's own direction, and by inversion (balance sheet up = more Fed absorption/QE-like = yield-suppressing, or if this reflects reduced runoff/passive growth, it is less duration supply pressure on the market, which is yield-negative). This is a genuine turn from the previous meeting, not a stale repeat. Term_premium also flipped from my prior -0.35 (falling) call to up +0.30 this meeting, which is directionally yield-positive on its own terms. Labor_tightness stayed up (yield-positive via tighter labor) though it's now 23 days stale — softening its weight somewhat but still directionally consistent with my prior lean. Inflation is flat and stale (16 days), consistent with prior near-zero view, so it stays a non-mover. Financial_conditions flat, fresh, no view change — consistent with prior. The key changes here are balance_sheet and term_premium, both now pointing toward higher yields rather than lower, directly contradicting my prior short-duration (long yield, betting yields fall) position. Term_premium's own gap acknowledgment (missing balance_sheet detail, missing financial_conditions read) tempers full conviction, but the balance_sheet analyst's fresh formed-today call is unambiguous and gives an independent yield-up signal. Given two of my prior bearish-yield anchors have reversed, I need to close out or substantially reduce my long-duration bet rather than hold it blindly.
